claim: 2026 comparison of Tailscale, Headscale, and NetBird; NetBird v0.65 (Feb 2026) added an integrated reverse proxy
quote: Since v0.65 (February 2026), NetBird includes a built-in reverse proxy – a feature that significantly shifts the comparison with Tailscale Funnel.

claim: NetBird teaches network thinking better than Tailscale for novice homelab users
quote: NetBird teaches you more about how your network actually functions, and for that reason, I think novice home-lab users would be better off using it over Tailscale.

claim: Tailscale handles CGNAT via DERP relays; raw WireGuard breaks behind CGNAT without a VPS
quote: If your ISP uses Carrier-Grade NAT (CGNAT), a standard WireGuard installation simply will not work without paying for an external VPS to bounce traffic through. Tailscale handles traversal automatically using the DERP relay network.

claim: Raw WireGuard requires manual port forwarding, key management, and peer config; tedious vs. Tailscale
quote: WireGuard offers raw speed, modern security, and 100% control, but needs manual port forwarding and a complex setup.

claim: Cloudflare Tunnel adds 15-45ms latency; Tailscale Funnel adds 10-80ms
quote: Cloudflare Tunnel adds 15-45ms latency while Tailscale Funnel adds 10-80ms; for high-throughput, Nginx on optimized VPS performs best, followed by Cloudflare Tunnel, then Tailscale Funnel.

claim: Tailscale Funnel is in beta, limited to ports 443/8443/10000, *.ts.net hostnames, with non-configurable bandwidth caps
quote: Funnel can only use DNS names in your tailnet's domain (tailnet-name.ts.net) and can only listen on ports 443, 8443, and 10000. Traffic sent over a Funnel is subject to non-configurable bandwidth limits.

claim: Pangolin is an open-source identity-aware reverse proxy and tunnel, AGPL-3 community edition free for personal use
quote: Identity-aware VPN and tunneled reverse proxy for remote access. Pangolin uses Traefik as a reverse proxy and exposes dynamic configuration that defines the routes/resources being exposed.

claim: Pangolin is a Y Combinator 2025 company providing a Cloudflare Tunnels alternative
quote: Show HN: Pangolin – Open source alternative to Cloudflare Tunnels. Fossorial is a Y Combinator 2025 company.

claim: 2026 comparison of Traefik, Caddy, NPM for homelabs; Caddy lightest, Traefik dynamic, NPM beginner-friendly
quote: Traefik, Caddy, and nginx Proxy Manager are the three most popular options in 2026 for homelab reverse proxies. Caddy (20-30 MB idle) is the lightest, followed by Traefik (40-60 MB), then NPM (80-120 MB).

claim: Recommendation: start with NPM, migrate to Traefik or Caddy as homelab grows
quote: If you are a beginner running a few services, start with Nginx Proxy Manager. If you want the cleanest configuration experience and automatic HTTPS, pick Caddy. If you want automation, multiple routers, and dynamic configuration driven by Docker labels, pick Traefik.

claim: Synology QuickConnect terminates TLS at quickconnect.to; in theory Synology can read credentials and traffic
quote: QuickConnect acts as a reverse proxy, and SSL certificates terminate at quickconnect.to. This means that, in theory, Synology could read the data sent across QuickConnect, including usernames and passwords.

claim: CGNAT shares one public IPv4 across customers, blocking inbound; workarounds include outbound tunnels, IPv6, or asking the ISP for a public IP
quote: Carrier-grade NAT (CGNAT) is when your ISP shares a single public IPv4 address across hundreds of customers. While outbound browsing still works, nobody on the internet can connect in to a server running at your address.

claim: NUT primary signals shutdown via FSD flag at critical battery; SHUTDOWNCMD triggers OS shutdown
quote: The upsmon primary notices the 'critical UPS' situation and sets 'FSD' — the 'forced shutdown' flag to tell all secondary systems that it will soon power down the load.

claim: NUT on a Raspberry Pi monitors UPS over USB and signals shutdown to multiple clients over the network
quote: NUT clients can then monitor the UPS through the Pi and safely shut down before the battery is depleted.

claim: Wake-on-LAN is unauthenticated; magic packets should be relayed via VPN, not exposed to the WAN
quote: WoL by itself is not secure—anyone who can send a magic packet can wake your PC. It's recommended to prefer a relay behind VPN or an authenticated HTTPS endpoint.

claim: A ~$25 smart plug acts as a hard-reset fallback when WoL/SSH fails; works regardless of OS state
quote: A $25 smart plug became my home lab's most reliable emergency fix. When all else fails, a smart plug is an incredibly effective fallback measure that does what you'd do in a hard situation — pull the plug and restart.

claim: PiKVM is the gold standard open-source IP-KVM with HDMI capture, USB keyboard/mouse emulation, virtual media, and ATX power control
quote: PiKVM is the most advanced open KVM over IP solution, providing BIOS-level remote control over servers.

claim: JetKVM is $69, 30-60 ms latency, 1080p60 video, built-in touchscreen, WebRTC remote
quote: JetKVM is priced at $69, offers 30-60ms latency with a built-in touchscreen, and includes free WebRTC-based remote access, though it lacks virtual media support and WiFi.

claim: iDRAC and iLO are full vendor BMC platforms; IPMI is the underlying standard; Redfish is the modern HTTPS/REST replacement
quote: iDRAC and iLO are full-fledged vendor remote management platforms with their own UI, licensing, security, telemetry, updates, and API, while IPMI is only the BMC management interface standard.

claim: N100 mini PCs draw 6-11 W idle; ~$12/yr US, $30/yr EU electricity; rule of thumb $1/W/yr US, $2.50/W/yr EU
quote: N100 mini PCs like the Beelink S12 Pro draw 6-8W at idle when headless, and 9-11W with mixed workloads. For homelab workloads, every watt of idle power consumption costs roughly $1/year at US rates or $2.50/year at EU rates.

claim: N100 mini PC + SSDs build idles 15-25 W; cost-effective for 24/7 home server use vs. older desktops
quote: A well-built home server system idles at 15-30 watts. For ultra-efficient builds, a mid-range Intel N100 build with two SSDs can sit around 15-25 watts idle.

claim: Tailscale subnet routers expose a private LAN to the tailnet via --advertise-routes; one device on the LAN serves the whole subnet
quote: Subnet routers provide access to specific private subnets and enable tailnet devices to reach non-Tailscale devices within those subnets.

claim: Uptime Kuma is the most popular self-hosted monitoring tool with 86k stars, dozens of monitor types, web UI, and 20s minimum check interval
quote: A fancy self-hosted monitoring tool. Uptime Kuma supports very short 20-second monitoring intervals.

claim: Gatus uses YAML config-as-code, ~30 MB RAM, no DB; Uptime Kuma uses ~100 MB and a web UI
quote: Gatus takes a different approach: configuration as code. It is lightweight, uses less RAM than Uptime Kuma (~30MB vs ~100MB), has no database with all state in memory, and is simple to deploy.

claim: Beszel is a lightweight Go-binary server monitor with Docker stats, historical graphs, and alerts; agent uses <10 MB RAM
quote: Lightweight server monitoring with historical data, docker stats, and alerts. The agent uses under 10 MB of RAM and auto-discovers all Docker containers via the Docker socket.

claim: Headscale reimplements Tailscale's coordination server; works with official Tailscale clients
quote: Headscale replicates Tailscale's coordination protocol, allowing organizations to run official Tailscale clients against infrastructure they operate.

claim: NetBird is fully self-hostable WireGuard mesh with OIDC and integrated dashboard, signal, relay, coturn services
quote: NetBird Docker Compose stack: Dashboard, Management, Signal, Relay, and Coturn. Both the client and the control/coordination server can be self-hosted.
